# UC-22.1.48 · GDPR Purpose Limitation Enforcement Across Systems

## Description

Personal data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and not further processed incompatibly. This use case compares declared processing purposes in ROPA to observed data flows — e.g., HR exports to marketing tools — using purpose tags on integrations where available.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR, NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Document each integration's allowed downstream categories in `gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv`; (2) populate `dest_category` from proxy and CASB; (3) alert when HR or health-tagged sources send data to marketing-class destinations; (4) require DPO sign-off for exceptions; (5) integrate with access reviews.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv`, CIM Network_Traffic, DLP policy labels.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Document each integration's allowed downstream categories in `gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv`; (2) populate `dest_category` from proxy and CASB; (3) alert when HR or health-tagged sources send data to marketing-class destinations; (4) require DPO sign-off for exceptions; (5) integrate with access reviews.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-7d
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.app
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv app OUTPUT ropa_purpose dest_category_allowed
| eval incompatible=if(NOT match(dest_category_allowed, dest_category),1,0)
| where incompatible=1
| table src, dest, app, ropa_purpose, dest_category, dest_category_allowed, bytes_out
| sort - bytes_out
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Purpose Limitation Enforcement Across Systems (Art. 25(1), Art. 5(1)(b))** — Personal data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and not further processed incompatibly. This use case compares declared processing purposes in ROPA to observed data flows — e.g., HR exports to marketing tools — using purpose tags on integrations where available.

Documented **Data sources**: `gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv`, CIM Network_Traffic, DLP policy labels. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **incompatible** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where incompatible=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: app, bytes_out, dest, dest_category, dest_category_allowed, incompatible, ropa_purpose, src.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sankey (source purpose to destination class), Table (incompatible flows), Single value (open violations).

## SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-7d
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.app
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_integration_purpose_map.csv app OUTPUT ropa_purpose dest_category_allowed
| eval incompatible=if(NOT match(dest_category_allowed, dest_category),1,0)
| where incompatible=1
| table src, dest, app, ropa_purpose, dest_category, dest_category_allowed, bytes_out
| sort - bytes_out
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Sankey (source purpose to destination class), Table (incompatible flows), Single value (open violations).
